intro.kicker: Topic 20 of 22 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: When two compressed blocks are suddenly released, they fly apart with equal and opposite momenta (P = −P by Newton’s third law). Each block’s kinetic energy is entirely dissipated by friction before it stops. Since both blocks have the same momentum magnitude P and the same coefficient of friction μ, the stopping distance formula S = P²/(2μm²g) shows that S ∝ 1/m². The ratio of their stopping distances is therefore S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)². The heavier block stops sooner; the lighter block travels farther.

expectedQuestions.description: NEET direct formula question: given m₁ and m₂ (e.g., 1 kg and 2 kg), same μ and initial contact — find S₁/S₂. Also asked as: ‘if m₂ = 2m₁, the lighter block travels how many times farther?’ (Answer: 4 times.) Conceptual variant: which block stops first? (Heavier block, since S ∝ 1/m².)

timeRequired.description: 20 min for energy method derivation, 20 min on NEET MCQs. Core proof: S = P²/(2μm²g) — derive from ½mv² = μmgS with v = P/m. Then take ratio. The derivation is short and elegant; the key is recognising that P is equal and opposite for both blocks.

difficulty.description: Students often confuse equal momentum with equal kinetic energy. For a compressed system: momentum is equal in magnitude (P = mv), but kinetic energy KE = P²/2m differs — lighter block has MORE KE. The formula S ∝ 1/m² (not 1/m) surprises many students. Watch for m² vs m in the denominator.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers momentum and friction but not this exact linked derivation. The step of expressing v = P/m and substituting into KE = μmgS to get S = P²/(2μm²g) is a NEET-specific derivation. NRI students should practise deriving the ratio formula from first principles rather than memorising the final ratio.

weightage.sectionTitle: Stopping of Two Blocks — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears every 2–3 years; 1 question per appearance.
Tested as ratio S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)² or mass ratio given S ratio.
Conceptual variant: equal momentum but unequal kinetic energy — lighter block has more KE.

strategySteps.title: Derive S = P²/(2μm²g) from First Principles
strategySteps.description: Start with KE = ½mv² = μmgS. Substitute v = P/m: ½·m·(P/m)² = μmgS ⇒ P²/(2m) = μmgS ⇒ S = P²/(2μm²g). So S ∝ 1/m² for same P and μ.

strategySteps.title: Derive the Ratio S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)²
strategySteps.description: S₁/S₂ = [P²/(2μm₁²g)] / [P²/(2μm₂²g)] = m₂²/m₁² = (m₂/m₁)². The heavier block (m₂ > m₁) has a smaller stopping distance.

strategySteps.title: Apply to Numerical Problems
strategySteps.description: m₁ = 1 kg, m₂ = 2 kg: S₁/S₂ = (2/1)² = 4. The lighter block stops 4x farther. Check: does result make physical sense? Lighter block has more KE (½m₁v₁² vs ½m₂v₂²: since P same, v₁ = 2v₂, KE₁ = 2×KE₂) but also less friction force (μm₁g < μm₂g). Both effects reinforce larger S₁.

cards.title: Two-Block Stopping Problem Notes
cards.description: Full derivation of S = P²/(2μm²g), ratio S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)², comparison with single-block stopping formulas, and 6 solved NEET-pattern MCQs covering ratio, reverse (given ratio, find mass ratio), and conceptual variants.

cards.title: Friction Stopping Distances — Master Summary
cards.description: All three stopping distance formulas: S = u²/(2μg) (velocity given), S = P²/(2μm²g) (momentum given), and inclined-plane variants. NEET quick-reference sheet.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Stopping of Two Blocks

cards.title: Stopping Distance Ratio for Equal-Momentum Blocks
cards.coreRevision: Two compressed blocks released: momenta P₁ = −P₂ (magnitudes equal). Both experience friction coefficient μ on same surface. Energy dissipated by friction: μmgS = ½mv² = P²/(2m). So S = P²/(2μm²g). Both blocks have same |P| and same μ: S ∝ 1/m². Therefore S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)².
points: Compressed system: |P₁| = |P₂| = P (momentum conservation from rest)
S ∝ 1/μ: more friction ⇒ shorter stopping distance
cards.example: m₁ = 2 kg, m₂ = 4 kg, same μ, released from compressed spring. S₁/S₂ = (4/2)² = 4. Lighter (2 kg) block stops 4 times farther than heavier (4 kg) block.

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics compares to NEET on two-block stopping distance.

gaps.title: Equal-Momentum Stopping Ratio Not Covered in AP Physics
gaps.description: AP Physics covers both friction and momentum independently but does not combine them in the compressed-spring release scenario to derive the S ∝ 1/m² result. The derivation using S = P²/(2μm²g) and the ratio (m₂/m₁)² is NEET-specific and requires deliberate practice for NRI students.
bullets: Key derivation step: substitute v = P/m into ½mv² = μmgS to get S = P²/(2μm²g).
Mass appears squared in denominator — a common mistake is writing 1/m instead of 1/m².

questions.question: Two blocks of masses 1 kg and 2 kg are compressed against each other and released on a rough surface (μ = 0.3). The ratio of their stopping distances S₁ : S₂ (1 kg : 2 kg) is:
questions.explanation: S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)² = (2/1)² = 4. So S₁ : S₂ = 4 : 1. The lighter 1 kg block, having the same momentum but less mass, travels four times farther before stopping.

questions.question: When two blocks compressed and released, the block with greater mass:

options.text: Travels farther since it has more momentum

questions.explanation: Both blocks have equal magnitude momenta. S = P²/(2μm²g): larger m ⇒ smaller S. The heavier block stops in a shorter distance. It also has smaller velocity (v = P/m) and smaller KE (= P²/2m), confirming it has less energy to dissipate.

questions.question: Two blocks of masses 3 kg and 6 kg are released from a compressed spring. S₁/S₂ (3 kg : 6 kg) = ?
For the two-block stopping problem, S ∝ ?
Block A (4 kg) and Block B (1 kg) released from compression. Which has more kinetic energy at release?
options: Block A (4 kg), because it has more mass
Block B (1 kg), because it has more velocity
questions.answer: Block B (1 kg), because it has more velocity

items.title: Recognise the compressed-block momentum setup
items.description: If two blocks are held compressed and released from rest, they have equal and opposite momenta. The system momentum is zero (conservation from rest). Each block has magnitude P = m₁v₁ = m₂v₂.

items.title: Derive or recall S = P²/(2μm²g)
items.description: From work-energy: μmgS = ½mv² = P²/(2m) ⇒ S = P²/(2μm²g). Note the m² in the denominator. This is different from the velocity-given formula S = v²/(2μg).

items.title: Apply the ratio S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)²
items.description: Under equal P and equal μ conditions, stopping distances are inversely proportional to mass squared. Heavier block ⇒ shorter stopping distance. Remember: it is mass ratio SQUARED, not just mass ratio.

items.question: Why is the stopping distance proportional to 1/m² and not 1/m?
items.answer: Because momentum P = mv is fixed, the velocity is v = P/m. KE = ½mv² = ½m(P/m)² = P²/(2m). The friction force is f = μmg. Stopping distance S = KE/f = [P²/(2m)] / (μmg) = P²/(2μm²g). Mass appears in both the KE (as 1/m) and the friction force (as m), giving m² in the denominator overall. Hence S ∝ 1/m².

items.question: What if the two blocks have different coefficients of friction?
items.answer: If μ₁ ≠ μ₂, then S₁/S₂ = (μ₂m₂²)/(μ₁m₁²). Both μ and m² appear in the ratio. The special case S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)² applies ONLY when both blocks are on the same surface (μ₁ = μ₂).

items.question: The lighter block has more kinetic energy, yet S ∝ 1/m² — is that consistent?
items.answer: Yes. KE = P²/(2m): lighter block has more KE (more energy to dissipate). But friction force = μmg is also smaller for a lighter block (less friction). The net effect: lighter block’s extra energy outweighs its reduced friction, so it stops farther. The factor is S₁/S₂ = (m₂/m₁)², not (m₂/m₁) as intuition might suggest.

grounding_summary: Page 244 presents the Stopping of Two Blocks scenario. Two masses are compressed towards each other and released. The momentum is equal and opposite for both blocks. The kinetic energy of each block is dissipated against friction until both stop. Since P and mu are the same for both blocks, S is proportional to 1/m^2, giving S1/S2 = (m2/m1)^2.
The textbook explicitly writes: In the given condition P and mu are same for both the blocks. So, S ∝ 1/m²; S₁/S₂ = [m₂/m₁]². This is the verbatim formula presented in the OCR source.
grounding_excerpt: When two masses compressed towards each other and suddenly released then energy acquired by each block will be dissipated against friction and finally block comes to rest
the person remains stuck to the wall even when the floor is removed, because friction force balances its weight in this condition.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Friction and Stopping Distance)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for friction stopping distance derivations, foundational for the two-block stopping distance ratio.

sources.title: NEET Physics Class 11 Chapter 6 Friction Notes — ClassNotes.guru
sources.why_used: NEET-focused notes confirming S1/S2 = (m2/m1)^2 as a standard NEET result for compressed-block problems.

review.notes: Content grounded on page 244 OCR. Formula S1/S2=(m2/m1)^2 derived from S=P^2/(2mu*m^2*g). Heavier block stops sooner confirmed. Three grounding excerpts sourced as verbatim substrings from page-244 OCR.
